data.title: Reaction Roundup: SA analysts, experts weigh in on consumer inflation report
data.description:
Wall Street's focus on Thursday was on the September U.S. consumer inflation report, in which both the headline number and the core figure came in hotter than expected on a M/M basis.

The data indicated that inflation risks still remain a concern.

According to the U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, headline consumer price index (CPI) rose 0.2% M/M in September, compared to a consensus figure of +0.1%. Core CPI inched up 0.3%, versus an expectation of +0.2%.

On a Y/Y basis, the situation came in mixed. Headline CPI increased 2.4%, higher than the anticipated number of +2.3% but decelerating from August's +2.5% reading. Core CPI climbed 3.3%, higher than the estimate of +3.2%.

U.S. stocks (SP500) opened lower after the data, with a higher-than-expected initial jobless claim reading also weighing on sentiment. Here are some exchange-traded funds that track the benchmark index: (SPY), (VOO), (IVV), (RSP), (SSO), (UPRO), (SH), (SDS), and (SPXU).

See below for various reactions to the CPI report:

Chris Lau, investing group leader of DIY Value Investing:

"Food and shelter accounted for most of the increase in inflation (75%). As expected, inflation rose in motor vehicle insurance, apparel, and airline fares. The 16.3% rise in insurance in the year benefited firms like Allstate (ALL) and Chubb (CB). Consumers could not escape food inflation, where prices rose in five out of six categories (meats, poultry, fish, eggs, fruits and vegetables). This squeezes profits for Conagra (CAG). It potentially pressures Coca-Cola (KO) and Pepsi (PEP).

Medical care costs increased, which may pressure the government to demand lower drug prices. Watch Elevance (ELV), UnitedHealth (UNH), Cigna (CI), and CVS Health (CVS).

The report benefited from a 1.9% drop in energy prices and -6.8% for the year. The gas index fell by 4.1%. In contrast, electricity prices rose, partly because AI servers increased demand.

After WTI crude prices strengthened in recent weeks, investors need to look ahead. As a hedge, investors may consider energy stocks: Exxon Mobil (XOM), Chevron (CVX), ConocoPhillips (COP), Devon Energy (DVN), and Occidental Petroleum (OXY)."

Michael Kramer, founder of Mott Capital Management and investing group leader of Reading The Markets:

"As swap pricing had suggested, the CPI report came in hotter than expected. The bigger surprise was the 0.3% month-over-month increase in core inflation, even as shelter inflation slipped. However, the real shock of the day may have been the surge in initial jobless claims, which now seems to be driving the bond and currency markets."

Victor Dergunov, investing group leader of The Financial Prophet:

"The CPI reading was higher than expected. It’s not the best gauge in my view, but unfortunately the market pays close attention to it. Hopefully, the market can shake it off, but the concern is that it may use it as an excuse for some short-term pre-earnings selling."

​Lael Brainard, U.S. National Economic Advisor:

"Today’s report shows inflation has fallen back down to 2.4%, the same rate as right before the pandemic. We keep making progress, with inflation returning to pre-pandemic levels, 16 million jobs created, lower interest rates, and low unemployment. Our economy has grown 3.2% per year under the Biden Harris Administration—stronger than during the previous administration. Incomes are up almost $4,000, after adjusting for inflation.

We are working around the clock to help the families affected by Hurricane Milton and Hurricane Helene recover and rebuild, supported by our strong and resilient recovery."

Jason Furman, former deputy director of the U.S. National Economic Council:

"Overall the same story as recently: IF you believe the last six months of numbers are correct and everything before that is outdated or anomalous then we're still in fine shape.

But if some of the last six months is anomalous (in reverse) then inflation is more of a concern.

An argument for keeping rates restrictive. But also consistent with them being less restrictive than they were given that whatever upside risk remains for inflation it is much smaller than it used to be.

So on track for 25bp at the next FOMC meeting."

Justin Wolfers, professor at the University of Michigan's economics department:

"My favorite thing about the inflation report is that it's become boring again.

Headline CPI rose 0.2% in September, and 2.4% over the year. In English, that's: Back to normal, low, and dull.

Core CPI rose 0.3% last month, up 3.3% over the year, and is still trending down."

Sarah House, senior economist at Wells Fargo:

"Today's data bring the year-ago change in the core CPI to 3.3%, with prices over the past three months increasing at a 3.1% annualized rate. For context, core CPI inflation averaged 2.2% in 2019, suggesting that the underlying pace of inflation at present is about one percentage point above what prevailed before the pandemic.

Looking ahead, we expect the disinflation trend to continue, albeit gradually rather than sharply. The ongoing cooling in the labor market and lagging service sector components should help reduce core inflation a bit further in the months ahead.

As a result, we expect the FOMC to continue normalizing monetary policy. We still anticipate two 25 bps rate cuts from the Federal Reserve at the two remaining FOMC meetings of the year."

More on the markets

  • Soft U.S. Headline CPI Is Unlikely To Be Sufficient To Reanimate Expectations Of Another Large Fed Cut
  • Investor Risk Aversion Eases, Though Lingers, In October
  • Dollar Vs. Yen: A Race To The Bottom
  • S&P 500 Q3 2024 Earnings Preview: Energy Faces Headwinds
  • S&P ends at record high, Dow up 1% after Fed minutes show majority backed 50bp rate cut

data.title: Oil prices rise as markets weigh possibility of Israeli attack on Iran
data.description:
Oil prices advanced on Thursday to partly offset a two-day decline as markets awaited Israel’s possible military response to Iran’s missile attack. U.S. stockpiles of crude increased the most in six months.

Brent (CO1:COM) rose above $77 a barrel and West Texas Intermediate (CL1:COM) approached $74 a barrel.

Traders are uneasy after Israeli Defense Minister Yoav Gallant on Wednesday said the country’s response “will be deadly, precise and above all surprising,” while Iran has warned it’s ready to launch thousands of missiles if needed.

Amid these tensions, countries in the oil-rich Persian Gulf region are lobbying Washington to prevent Israel from attacking Iran’s oil sites, Reuters reported Thursday.

Countries such as Saudi Arabia, the United Arab Emirates and Qatar are concerned that their oil facilities will face retaliation from Iran’s proxies. The countries also are refusing to allow Israel to fly into their airspace in any aerial attack on Iran, Reuters reported.

President Joe Biden has discouraged attacks on Iran’s oil infrastructure. On Wednesday, he spoke with Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu for the first time in over a month.

Markets on Thursday were steady after a report showed that inflation was greater than had been forecast in the United States. Investors are seeking indications that inflation and economic growth will be strong enough to dissuade the Federal Reserve from making significant cuts to borrowing costs.

data.title: InMode cuts FY revenue guidance
data.description:
• InMode (NASDAQ:INMD), a provider of medical technologies, said on Thursday that it expects Q3 revenue in the range of $130M -$130.1M.
  • The company now expects Non-GAAP gross margin to be in the range of 81% to 82% and decreased it FY 2024 revenue guidance to $410M-$420M.
  • Referring to the currency situation in Israel, InMode said it is proactively assessing the relocation of certain production activities outside of Israel.

data.title: Miivo Holdings announces private placement of up to C$1M
data.description:
• Miivo Holdings (TSXV:MIVO:CA) has announced a non brokered private placement of up to 5M units of the Co. at a price of $0.20/Unit for aggregate gross proceeds of up to C$1M.
  • Each Unit will consist of one common share and one-half (½) of a Common Share purchase warrant of the Co.
  • Each Warrant will be exercisable to acquire one Common Share for a period of 12 months following the closing date of the Offering at an exercise price of C$0.40/Common Share.
  • The net proceeds from the Offering are expected to be used for investments and for general corporate purposes.

data.title: American Tower cut to Equal Weight as Wells Fargo steps back from tower REITs
data.description:
Wells Fargo Equity Research downgraded American Tower (NYSE:AMT) shares to Equal Weight from Overweight on Thursday as analyst Eric Luebchow moved to the sidelines across telecom tower REITs.

For the overall subsector, Luebchow sees a slow leasing environment over the near-term, with challenged AFFO growth until 2026.

Also stoking the downgrade, he wrote in a note, American Tower (AMT) stock is trading at a ~3x-5x premium to peers on a EBITDA-to-FFO ratio. Seeking Alpha's Quant system gives AMT a valuation grade of "D-," with the poorest marks in price-to-book and total-debt-to-equity.

"While multi-year growth for AMT is more favorable through 2027 vs. peers, reported AFFO likely to be relatively flat in 2025 (India dilution, modest refinancing headwinds + LatAm churn)," the note said.

Wells Fargo's Equal Weight rating agrees with the Quant rating of Hold and diverges from the average SA analyst rating and the average Wall Street analyst rating, both at Buy.

(AMT) slipped 0.8% minutes after the opening bell.

data.title: China arrests four Taiwanese workers at Foxconn Apple plant: report
data.description:
• Chinese authorities arrested four Taiwanese employees who work at a Foxconn (OTCPK:FXCOF) factory in Zhengzhou, which serves as the largest assembly plant for Apple (NASDAQ:AAPL) iPhones, according to a report Thursday by the Wall Street Journal.
  • Authorities provided a vague reasoning for the arrests. The Taiwan Mainland Affairs council said the employees were accused of a breach of trust, according to the report.
  • The council also said the allegations were bizarre, and could lead to a loss of investor confidence in China, especially if the arrests are eventually deemed unjustified. It urged for a rapid and transparent investigation.
  • The Zhengzhou factory has been dubbed iPhone city. Foxconn said they do not expect any financial impact from the detentions.

data.title: Solventum appoints Ryan Egeland as CMO
data.description:
• Solventum (NYSE:SOLV) has announced that Dr. Ryan Egeland, MD, has been named CMO.
  • In this role, he will focus on fostering industry collaboration and identifying innovation and market expansion opportunities at the intersection of scientific rigor, clinical need, and commercial practicality.
  • Dr. Egeland will report to Chris Barry, executive vice president and group president, Medical Surgical, Solventum.

data.title: Nasdaq, S&P, Dow fall as CPI rises more than expected, jobless claims comes in hot
data.description:
Wall Street's major averages slid on Thursday, as September consumer inflation comes in hotter than expected.

Early on and the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was -0.4%, the S&P 500 (SP500) was -0.2%, and the Dow (DJI) was -0.2%.

The 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) was up 1 basis point to 4.08%. The 2-year yield (US2Y) was down 4 basis points to 3.98%.

Major U.S. stock averages closed in the green on Wednesday, with the S&P 500 (SP500) and the Dow (DJI) ending at new peaks after the Federal Reserve's September meeting minutes said a "substantial majority" on its rate-setting board backed its big half-point cut to start an easing cycle.

"Markets have put in another strong performance over the last 24 hours, as optimism about the near-term economic outlook continues to build," Deutsche Bank's Henry Allen said.

"Reading the (FOMC) Minutes, CPI is important to what happens next," Economist Steven Blitz from Global Data TS Lombard said.

September Consumer Price Index +0.2% M/M vs. +0.1% expected and +0.2% prior, +2.4% Y/Y vs. +2.3% expected and +2.5% prior.

Core CPI, which excludes food and energy: +0.3% M/M vs. +0.2% expected and +0.3% prior, +3.3% Y/Y vs. +3.2% expected and +3.2% prior.

The CPI report is in particular focus after the strong jobs report last Friday, as there’s been growing speculation that the Fed might not cut at all at their next meeting in November, Allen added.

The initial jobless claims climbed more than expected in the past week.

The Fed balance sheet will come later in the day.

data.title: GoHealth appoints Brendan Shanahan as CFO
data.description:
• GoHealth (NASDAQ:GOCO) on Thursday announced the appointment of Brendan Shanahan as chief financial officer (CFO), effective October 14.
  • Shanahan, who holds over 20 years of expertise in the Medicare Advantage space, will manage all financial aspects of the digital health company.
  • In addition, the company said Katherine O'Halloran will continue to serve as chief accounting officer and reporting to the CFO.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: First Solar trades lower as Jefferies pares third quarter expectations on potential delays
data.description:
Shares of First Solar (NASDAQ:FSLR) traded lower in premarket on Thursday as brokerage Jefferies pared its expectations for FSLR's upcoming third-quarter results.

Jefferies expected the focus this earnings season to be on potential delays, impact on module pricing from AD/CVD determinations, and thoughts on selling excess Indian capacity into the U.S.

"Going into 3Q, we expect a slight miss with lower volume versus consensus. We also lower volume estimates for 2025/2026, which are 8%/4% below consensus and are below 2024 guidance," said Jefferies' analysts.

With a 4Q weighted second half, Jefferies said there is always risk of projects being pushed into 1Q, and as a result, the brokerage slashed its 4Q and FY volumes and currently sits about 2% below consensus and at the lower end of the FY guidance.

Additionally, Jefferies did not expect significant backlog additions in 3Q. "We estimate a total backlog of 2GW in 3Q compared to a consensus of 3.2GW. We expect the momentum to resume in the first half of 2025."

Wall Street on average forecasts First Solar to post a quarterly EPS of about $3.20 along with a revenue of about $1.09 billion.

Since the start of the year, First Solar has gained about 31.2%. Seeking Alpha's Quant and sell-side analysts recommend the company has a Buy.
